coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of broadband red-emitting hydrophilic carbon dots for zebrafish imaging application

coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Wenjing
Zhang, Jiahui
Xiao, Jiayi
Zeng, Linyun
Li, Chunli
Zhou, Liya
Huang, Jinqing
Pan, Jialiang
Liu, Lihong
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: Red luminescence is crucial in bio-applications because of its high penetration and low photo-damage to tissue. Hydrophobicity is a major drawback to most of the reported red-emitting carbon dots (CDs), which limits their application due to poor dispersion and low quantum efficiency in aqueous phase. To solve this problem, we prepared carbon dots with broadband red emission using a simple hydrothermal process, using Congo red and m-phenylenediamine as raw materials. CDs demonstrated a bright and excitation-independent emission at ∼600 nm with FWHM of 125 nm. The CDs aqueous solution shows bright red luminescence with PLQY of 6.13 %. Meanwhile, the CDs exhibit good properties like salt tolerance, anti-photobleaching, and low cytotoxicity. As a proof-of-concept experiment, CDs were utilized to cultivate zebrafish embryos and larvae, displaying clear fluorescence images of uptake and metabolism, which further demonstrates its potential in biological imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission
Carbon dots
Hydrophilic
Optical properties
Biological imaging

The corresponding author would like to express his thanks to Prof. Lihong Liu and Prof. Jialiang Pan for providing research resource, and to two lovely and naughty sons (Zhiping Zhang & Zhibang Zhang) who always give inspiration and responsibility to their father.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL spectra of products synthesized by Congo red with different nitrogen dopants; (b) PLE spectra of CDs synthesized by Congo Red with MPD at various molar ratios; (c) PL spectra of CR-CDs synthesized at different temperatures; (d) PL spectra of CR-CDs synthesized at different reaction times; (e) PL spectra of CR-CDs at different concentrations; (f) Photos of products synthesized by Congo red with different nitrogen dopant.
(a) TEM images and (b) HR-TEM image of CR-CDs showing particle size distribution and lattice spacing.
(a) FTIR Spectra of CR-CDs, CDs, and MPD; (b) Full-survey scanning XPS spectrum High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) N 1 s and (f) S 2p of CR-CDs.
(a) Absorption, PL and PLE spectra; (b) Decay curve monitoring 600 nm emission at 450 nm excitation; (c) Excitation-dependent 3D PL spectra (d) CIE diagram of CR-CDs.
PL spectra of CR-CDsat 450 nm excitation under various NaCl concentrations (a),UV irradiation times (b, temperatures (c), cation & anions(d) and pH conditions (e).
(a) Survival rate of zebrafish larvae and (b) that of zebrafish embryos.
Brightfield and fluorescence images of 4hpf zebrafish embryos under different concentrations of CR-CDs and incubation times.
Bright-field and fluorescence images of 6dpf zebrafish larvae under different concentrations of CR-CDs and incubation times.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of broadband red-emitting hydrophilic carbon dots for zebrafish imaging application

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red luminescence is crucial in bio-applications because of its high penetration and low photo-damage to tissue. Hydrophobicity is a major drawback to most of the reported red-emitting carbon dots (CDs), which limits their application due to poor dispersion and low quantum efficiency in aqueous phase. To solve this problem, we prepared carbon dots with broadband red emission using a simple hydrothermal process, using Congo red and m-phenylenediamine as raw materials. CDs demonstrated a bright and excitation-independent emission at ∼600 nm with FWHM of 125 nm. The CDs aqueous solution shows bright red luminescence with PLQY of 6.13 %. Meanwhile, the CDs exhibit good properties like salt tolerance, anti-photobleaching, and low cytotoxicity. As a proof-of-concept experiment, CDs were utilized to cultivate zebrafish embryos and larvae, displaying clear fluorescence images of uptake and metabolism, which further demonstrates its potential in biological imaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) typically refer to carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials with dimensions <10 nm and a core-shell structure. Due to their unique optical properties, CDs hold significant potential in the biological field, with a broad range of applications including drug delivery, bio-sensing, antimicrobial treatments, and anticancer therapies, particularly in bio-imaging [1–9]. Although many kinds of CDs had been reported so far, most of them show intense emission only at blue- to green-light regions under excitation with ultraviolet- or blue-light. Such drawbacks restrict their further applications [10–12]. It is highly desirable to achieve high-efficient red-light emissive CDs that can be excited with long wavelength lights as well. It is known that a lower energy transitions generally require more conjugated structures or special surface modifications, which result in more severe non-radiative loss. Meanwhile, certain critical problems still exist including very low quantum yields (QYs), and complicated synthesis/separation procedures. There are many types of red emitting CDs have been reported in recent years, but most of them are hydrophobic, which could be quickly discharged from water, accumulate excessively in issue, suffer severe luminescence quenching in aqueous solution, and thus not suitable for bio-medical applications [13–15].

Hydrophilic groups can increase the water dispersity of the CDs, facilitating cell and biological experiments. It is known that the optical and surface properties of CDs are closely related to the type of precursors, reaction media, and synthesis conditions [16–19]. The surface functional groups and electronic structure of CDs can be effectively adjusted by carefully choosing carbon sources and synthesis pathways, thereby improving and optimizing their luminescence performance, especially in aqueous solution. Hydrophilic groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl on CDs can be generated by using hydrothermal methods and proper water-soluble precursors. Congo-red (CR, C32H22N6Na2O6S2) is an azo dye known for its low cost, good solubility, and stability in water, which makes it widely used in the pulp, paper, textile, and pigment industries. However, CR and its metabolites pose significant toxicity to the environment and living organisms [1]. In present days, the production concept of green chemistry has gradually become popular, thus the transformation of chemical waste into high-value-added functional materials is a hot research spot. By converting CR into carbon dots, the waste dyes can be effectively recycled while environmental pollution can be greatly reduced. This method employs a one-step hydrothermal reaction to directly synthesize red carbon dots using Congo Red and m-phenylenediamine, eliminating the need for complex multi-step processes or additional surface modifications. Furthermore, the synthesis utilizes readily available and cost-effective precursors (Congo Red and m-phenylenediamine), making it more economical compared to other methods that rely on rare or expensive raw materials.

Fluorescence imaging technology is crucial in biological imaging due to its high sensitivity, ease of observation, and simple instrumentation. Studies have explored the application of CD-based fluorescence imaging both in vitro (e.g., in cancer cells, neuronal cells, and organelles) and in vivo (e.g., in mice and zebrafish) [20]. Zebrafish are particularly valuable for bio-imaging studies due to their high genetic homology with humans, the relevance of zebrafish disease models to human conditions, and their advantageous characteristics such as small size, high reproductive capability, and ease of maintenance [21,22]. Additionally, the transparency of zebrafish embryos and larvae allows for direct observation of internal structures and organ development through a microscope, providing excellent conditions for bio-imaging research. By referring to our previous works on CDs for imaging applications [16,18,19], we find that it is critical to further develop red emitting CDs with strong luminescence, good water dispersity and high stability.

In this study, a new type of red-emitting CDs was prepared by hydrothermal synthesis route using Congo red and m-phenylenediamine as raw materials, referred to as Congo red CDs (CR-CDs). The synthesized CR-CDs exhibit remarkable optical properties, with a maximum excitation wavelength of 450 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 600 nm, accompanied by a full width at half maxima (FWHM) of 125 nm. The CR-CDs exhibit an absolute quantum yield of 6.13 % in an aqueous solution. They also possess excellent properties such as water solubility, good biocompatibility, high stability and low toxicity, highlighting their significant potential for imaging applications. To validate the bio-imaging application potential, CR-CDs were applied to zebrafish embryos and larvae. These results further support the potential of CR-CDs for bio-imaging applications.

In this study, a hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize red fluorescent carbon dots using Congo Red (CR) and m-Phenylenediamine (MPD) as precursors with water as the solvent. The optimal synthesis steps are as follows: 0.4182 g of Congo red (0.6 mmol) and 0.1944 g of MPD (1.8 mmol) were weighed in a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined container. 30 mL of water was added as the solvent. After ultrasonication for 5 min, the lined container was transferred to a stainless steel high-temperature reaction vessel and kept in a 220 °C air oven for 4 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the product was purified by filtering through a 220 nm microporous filter membrane.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Infrared (IR) spectra were acquired using a Bruker TENSOR II Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) data were acquired using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) characterization was performed using a JEOL JEM-F200 microscope, manufactured in Japan. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy measurements were carried out using a UV-6100 double-beam scanning UV–Vis spectrophotometer from MAPADA. Photoluminescence (PL), Photoluminescence Excitation (PLE), and fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using an Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer. Absorbance measurements were performed using a Multiskan FC microplate photometer from Thermo Fisher Scientific. Fluorescence imaging of zebrafish embryos and larvae was conducted using the Zeiss Axio Observer A1 inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:para: Two female and three male mature zebrafish were dark-adapted for 13 h before mating to produce embryos. The embryos were repeatedly washed and then placed in a culture medium for 4 h. In a 6-well plate, CR-CDs were added at concentrations of 2 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL, 0.25 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, 0.125 mg/mL, and 0.1 mg/mL. Zebrafish embryos, which had been incubated for 4 h, were then introduced into the wells and observed for 1 to 6 h. The embryos were repeatedly washed to remove excess CR-CDs, and imaging was performed using an inverted microscope. For imaging zebrafish larvae after 6 d of incubation, the same method was used at the same CR-CDs concentrations. The zebrafish larvae were anesthetized with MS-222 (diluted 2000-fold) before observation.

As shown in Fig. 1a andf, hydrothermal products synthesized by Congo red with different nitrogen dopant were synthesized at 200 °C for 4 h. The product from solo Congo Red exhibits no fluorescence after hydrothermal treatment. However, after doped with different nitrogen sources: MPD, o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), and p-Phenylenediamine (PPD), obvious red fluorescence could be observed in all samples. Notably, the intensity of the PPD-doped sample is relatively weak, whereas the MPD- and OPD-doped samples exhibit stronger light. In addition, the MPD-doped sample shows a slight red shift in fluorescence, and the color of the synthesized solutions (Fig. 1f) corresponds to the spectral changes. These observations indicate that the molecular structures formed by different nitrogen dopants significantly influence the electronic transitions and conjugation characteristics of the carbon dots systems. The differences in optical and fluorescence properties are primarily attributed to the electronic effects and spatial configurations of the nitrogen dopants. MPD, with its symmetric structure and strong electron-donating ability, enhances π-π stacking and electron delocalization, resulting in optimal fluorescence performance. In contrast, the asymmetric structure of OPD and the para-configuration of PPD diminish these effects, leading to weaker optical responses. Thus, MPD was identified as the optimal dopant in this study. Subsequently, further optimization experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 1b, The optimal CR: MPD molar ratio is 1:3. Fig. 1c explores the effect of various hydrothermal temperatures with CR:MPD molar ratio of 1: 3. Finally, Fig. 1d investigates the influence of reaction time at a fixed CR:MPD molar ratio of 1:3 and a synthesis temperature of 220 °C. The optimal conditions were MPD as the dopant, a CR: MPD ratio of 1:3, and a synthesis temperature of 220 °C for 4 h.

The morphology and size distribution of CR-CDs are illustrated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2a , CR-CDs are well dispersed in aqueous solutions, revealing uniform dispersion and quasi-spherical morphology. The average particle size of CR-CDs was determined to be 3.10 nm through calculations. The high-resolution TEM image (HR-TEM, Fig. 2b) shows that at a size of 2 nm, the lattice spacing of the CDs is approximately 0.218 nm, which can be assigned to the (100) plane of graphene, suggesting that G-CDs possess a graphene-like layered structure.

The surface functional groups of CR-CDs and raw materials like CR, MPD were analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 3a ). CR-CDs exhibited a broad absorption band in the range of 3070–3656 cm−1, attributed to OH/NH stretching vibrations. A peak at 1625 cm−1 was assigned to C=O/C=N stretching vibrations, while the peak at 1496 cm−1 corresponded to C=C bending vibrations. CN stretching vibrations were observed at 1313 cm−1, and -SO₃H vibrations were detected at 1112 cm−1. The region between 600 and 900 cm−1 was attributed to aromatic ring breathing vibrations. Additionally, the peak at 615 cm−1 was associated with C-S vibrations. Compared with the FT-IR spectra of CR and MPD, the broadening of the OH/NH peak in CR-CDs indicates the introduction of abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface. The C=O/C=N vibrations, which were not prominent in CR or MPD, appeared distinctly in CR-CDs, suggesting the introduction of carbonyl or imine groups during the synthesis process. Furthermore, the retention of partial aromatic structures in CR-CDs was confirmed [23]. To further investigate the surface composition of CR-CDs sample, the XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3b indicates that CR-CDs are composed of sulfur (S), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), oxygen (O), and sodium (Na), with atomic percentages of 1.22 %, 64.66 %, 11.53 %, 16.31 %, and 6.28 %, respectively. Both nitrogen and oxygen contents in CR-CDs are high, suggesting their good hydrophilicity. By deconvoluting the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and S 2p, more detailed information about the surface chemical states of CR-CDs can be obtained. S 2p (168 eV), C 1 s (284 eV), N 1 s (398 eV), O 1 s (530 eV), and Na 1 s (1070 eV), with the peak at 500 eV identified as the Sodium Auger peak (Na KLL), corresponding to the high-resolution XPS results. The C1 s peak (Fig. 3c) is deconvoluted into four components, i.e., 283.1 eV (C=C/CC), 283.9 eV (CN), 284.6 eV (CO/C–S) and 287.7 eV (C=O). High-resolution O 1 s curve revealed the presence of two peaks at 530.7 eV and 534.7 eV, originating from C=O and S=O/S-O/CO, respectively (Fig. 3d). The N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3e) clearly exhibited two peaks around 397.6 eV, and 398.2 eV, implying the existence of CN–C and NH. The S 2p band can be divided into two peaks at 166.5 eV and 167.7 eV (Fig. 3f), representing C-S-C and -SO2, respectively [23–25]. The presence of OH/NH stretching vibrations indicates hydrogen bonding potential, while C=O, C–N, S=O/S–O, and C–O groups all enhance the hydrophilic nature due to their ability to interact with water molecules through hydrogen bonding and dipole-dipole interactions. This increased hydrophilicity can significantly impact the solubility and interaction of CR-CDs in aqueous environments.

As shown in Fig. 4a , the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CR-CDs exhibits a broad absorption band in the range of 400∼550 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transitions of the C=N and C=O bonds [26]. Meanwhile, the PL and PL spectra in Fig. 4a reveal that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CR-CDs are 450 nm and 600 nm, respectively. The corresponding lifetime curve monitoring 600 nm at 450 nm excitation is exhibited in Fig. 4b, which could be well fitted by single exponential equation and the average lifetime value is 2.968 ns. The excitation-dependent 3D PL spectra of CR-CDs were measured and shown in Fig. 4c. It can be observed that the emission peak wavelength is nearly identical at 600 nm under different excitation wavelengths from 390 to 550 nm, exhibiting an excitation-independent emission behavior. Therefore, an excitation wavelength of 450 nm was set as the optimal value throughout further testing and application. The absolute QY value was measured in an integrating sphere at optimal excitation wavelength of 450 nm, where CR-CDs were dispersed in water with an absorbance value of ∼ 0.1. The QY value was found as 6.13 % under this condition. Fig. 4d depicts the CIE chromaticity diagram of CR-CDs emission, in which the corresponding CIE coordinates is (0.536, 0.452) and in the red region. The CR-CD aqueous solution is a clear yellow solution at sunlight, while a bright red emission can be clearly seen under 450 nm UV light with a 620 nm filter (Fig. 4d inset), indicating the good luminescence performance of CR-CDs.

In order to have a comprehensive study of the CR-CDs’ stability characteristics,a series of PL measurements were conducted under conditions of varying salt concentrations, light irradiation, temperature and substances. Firstly, the salt resistance of CR-CDs was studied with NaCl concentrations ranging from 0 to 1.8 mol/L. As shown in Fig. 5a , the PL spectra of CR-CDs remain stable even at high salt concentrations, confirming their ability to maintain good luminescence performance in high sodium ion environments. Secondly, the anti-photobleaching property of CR-CDs was analyzed in Fig. 5b No significant changes are observed in both the peak wavelength and emission intensity under continuous 365 nm UV irradiation for 80 min, demonstrating outstanding photostability. Furthermore, the stability of CR-CDs at various temperatures was investigated. The CR-CDs were dissolved in water with the absorbance of ∼0.1, and their PL spectra were measured at different temperature points. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence intensity increases with rising temperature while the change of peak wavelength is negligible, suggesting that CR-CDs remain stable in high temperature environments. Fig. 5d demonstrates that the fluorescence characteristics of CR-CDs are similar in different cations and anions (Al3+, As3+, Cd2+, Co2+, CrO4 2−, Cu2+, F−, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, PBS, Se4+, Zn2+). Last but not the least, the pH-dependent PL spectra of CR-CD are depicted in Fig. 5e. Under pH = 1 condition, the peak of CR-CDs exhibits a blue shift. When pH is below 3, the lower the pH, the more purple the solution appears under natural light. However, under other pH conditions, both the peak and the color remain unchanged. The fluorescence intensity of CR-CDs remains relatively stable within the pH range of 3 to 11. The above results show that CR-CDs own unique optical properties, excellent stability and anti-interference capability under different environmental conditions, making them a promising candidate as fluorescence probes in the biological imaging field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging application of CR-CDs in zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the similarity of homologous genes between zebrafish and humans, as well as its optical transparency, zebrafish serve as an excellent model vertebrate for fluorescent probes [27,28]. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, zebrafish embryos and larvae developed normally in the CR-CDs solution. Although at 0.5 mg/mL, the survival rate of embryos decreased to 50 % after 18 h of incubation, and the survival rate of larvae was 55 % after 24 h, at concentrations of 0.25 mg/mL or lower, zebrafish embryos maintained a survival rate above 80 % after 18 h, and zebrafish larvae maintained a 100 % survival rate even after 30 h of incubation. These results indicate that CR-CDs exhibit good biocompatibility.

Fig. 7 shows the bright-field and fluorescence images of 4 hpf zebrafish embryos captured under an inverted fluorescence microscope, which were incubated with CR-CDs at concentrations of 0.125, 0.2, 0.25, 0.50, 1.0 and 2.0 mg/mL, along with optimal 0.1 mg/mL for 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 h, respectively. Based on the previously mentioned PL spectrum, it can be concluded that the fluorescence intensity of CR-CDs is the strongest under 450 nm excitation. Under this excitation, the control group, in which the CR-CDs content is 0 mg/ml, exhibited no fluorescence under the microscope, thereby ruling out the possibility of fluorescence originating from the solvent or the nutrient solution. Meanwhile, the experimental groups incubated with CR-CDs display strong red fluorescence, indicating that CR-CDs successfully penetrated the chorion[29;30]. According to Fig. 6b, zebrafish embryos maintained a 100 % survival rate even after 3 h of incubation at a high CR-CDs concentration (2 mg/mL). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7, the embryos at this concentration exhibited an intact morphology and emitted bright fluorescence. Additionally, at a lower concentration of 0.25 mg/mL, the embryos displayed clear and intense fluorescence after 5 h of incubation. Notably, as both the CR-CDs concentration and incubation time increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually intensified, reaching its peak after 6 h of incubation at 2 mg/mL.

This study further investigated the imaging of CR-CDs in zebrafish larvae. Fig. 8 shows bright-field and fluorescence images of 6dpf zebrafish larvae incubated with CR-CDs at concentrations of 2 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL, 0.25 mg/mL, 0.2 mg/mL, 0.125 mg/mL, and 0.1 mg/mL for 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 h, respectively, under an inverted fluorescence microscope. The control group does not exhibit fluorescence at 450 nm excitation, while the CR-CDs-incubated zebrafish displays intense red fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 6a, even at a high concentration of 2 mg/mL, zebrafish larvae maintained a 100 % survival rate after 2 h of incubation, displaying bright and intact fluorescence imaging. Additionally, at a lower concentration of 0.25 mg/mL, clear and vivid fluorescence imaging of zebrafish larvae could be observed within just 4 h. The fluorescence intensity of CR-CDs increases with concentration, reaching its peak at 2 mg/mL. Similarly, in zebrafish larvae, fluorescence intensity continues to increase, reaching its maximum after 6 h of incubation with CR-CDs. Zebrafish larvae achieve high fluorescence intensity more readily in a shorter period compared to zebrafish embryos.

In this study, aqueous red-emitting carbon dots were synthesized via hydrothermal synthesis using Congo red and m-phenylenediamine as raw materials. The CR-CDs aqueous solution exhibits bright broadband red emission with a peak at 600 nm, full width at half FWHM of 125 nm, and a QY of 6.13 %. The optical properties of CR-CDs were thoroughly studied, revealing excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, salt resistance, and photo-bleaching resistance. Given their high biocompatibility and stable fluorescence properties, CR-CDs were applied to zebrafish embryos and larvae, where strong red fluorescence was observed. These results demonstrate that CR-CDs not only possess efficient fluorescence properties but also exhibit low biotoxicity, highlighting their potential and value in bio-imaging applications.

Wenjing Huang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiahui Zhang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Jiayi Xiao: Data curation, Conceptualization. Linyun Zeng: Formal analysis. Chunli Li: Investigation. Liya Zhou: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Jinqing Huang: Project administration. Jialiang Pan: Investigation. Lihong Liu: Project administration. Xinguo Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is funded by the Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA080005). The corresponding author would like to express his thanks to Prof. Lihong Liu and Prof. Jialiang Pan for providing research resource, and to two lovely and naughty sons (Zhiping Zhang & Zhibang Zhang) who always give inspiration and responsibility to their father.
